chunks.title: Core Story Requirements
chunks.content: Stories must include a minimum level of interactivity, educational structure, and dice-based uncertainty. Each story needs at least five choice scenes to keep all players engaged. Every character in the roster must receive at least one Action Choice (where they decide HOW to tackle a challenge) and at least one Challenge Roll that tests their abilities. Along any complete narrative path from start to ending, roughly a quarter of scenes should be roll-based when you count narrative, choice, roll, and special scenes together. The story must use a d20 for rolls, and the outcome of a choice branch and the outcome of a roll branch may not lead to the same next_scene id. Stories must support multiple endings driven by player decisions, and no branch should be a dead end: each branch must either move the plot forward, reveal character, or support an educational goal.
chunks.examples: At least five choice scenes; each character has an Action Choice and a Challenge Roll; about 25% of scenes along any full path are roll scenes; choice and roll branches cannot share a next_scene; multiple endings.

chunks.title: Educational Goals Overview
chunks.content: Each adventure must intentionally foster three major developmental domains: social and emotional growth, cognitive skills, and moral reasoning. Social-emotional goals include helping children name and understand their feelings, build competence and industry through age-appropriate challenges, practice empathy and friendship skills, and learn emotional regulation and resilience. Cognitive goals emphasize concrete logical thinking suited to the concrete operational stage, strengthening executive functions like working memory, cognitive flexibility, and inhibitory control, and encouraging creative, divergent thinking with multiple valid solutions. Moral goals focus on building moral integrity through simple ethical dilemmas, showing how actions affect others, and highlighting themes such as fairness, honesty, responsibility, community care, and prosocial behaviour.
chunks.examples: Stories that balance emotional growth, puzzles that require planning and flexible thinking, and dilemmas where children see how honesty or fairness affects others.

chunks.title: Gameplay Mechanics and Tone
chunks.content: Gameplay uses Focus Choices and Echo Logs to link key decisions to developmental tracking for caregivers, and can include symbolic in-story rewards and cosmetic collectibles stored on the caregiver profile. The narrative tone must be concise, clear, and engaging for ages 6–9, introducing slightly more complex vocabulary only when the meaning is clear from context. Humour is a required element and must be developmentally appropriate: jokes should come from wordplay, misunderstandings, or the absurdity of situations and should help bond the group. Humour must never rely on cruelty, humiliation, punching down, or harmful stereotypes. Overall tone should support emotional safety, curiosity, and growth, framing mistakes as learning opportunities instead of sources of shame.
chunks.examples: Using Focus Choices to log growth echoes, awarding a symbolic group reward at the end of a quest, and adding gentle, silly humour that makes the group laugh together without mocking anyone.

chunks.title: Narrative Causality and Consistency
chunks.content: The story world must obey strict internal logic so that children can follow cause and effect. Every scene should grow directly out of the actions, choices, or dice rolls of the previous scene, avoiding abrupt jump cuts that break the chain of events. Characters must act in line with their established abilities, motives, and emotional states; any growth or new skills should be shown or explained on-screen. Environmental rules, including magic systems, physics quirks, and cultural norms, must remain consistent unless the story explicitly shows them changing. Choices should have visible consequences that can ripple forward; delayed outcomes must eventually pay off so players can see the cause–effect loop. No branches should lead nowhere: each path should advance the plot, deepen character understanding, or support one of the educational goals.
chunks.examples: A rude choice causes a friend to be distant later; using a new spell requires prior learning; a risky shortcut taken earlier leads to a complication several scenes later.

chunks.title: Emotional Development (Self-Awareness and Self-Management)
chunks.content: Stories should support both self-awareness and self-management. For self-awareness, children practice identifying nuanced emotions such as pride, jealousy, disappointment, and gratitude, as well as moral emotions like guilt and righteous pride. They begin connecting feelings, thoughts, and values, recognising how group memberships shape identity, and developing a realistic sense of strengths and limitations that underpins industry and confidence. For self-management, stories should model managing emotions, using stress-management strategies such as calming routines or seeking help from trusted adults, and separating feelings, thoughts, and actions so children can pause before reacting. Characters can demonstrate examining their own biases before they turn into unkind behaviour, showing that it is possible to notice and redirect internal reactions.
chunks.examples: Scenes where a character names feeling jealous and then chooses a constructive response, or where a stressed character uses a calming plan before acting.

chunks.title: Social Skills (Social Awareness and Relationship Skills)
chunks.content: Stories should help children practice reading social cues, taking others’ perspectives, and building healthy relationships. Characters should demonstrate noticing how others feel, asking considerate questions, listening actively, and navigating everyday conflicts in age-appropriate ways. Plots should include situations where cooperation, sharing, turn-taking, and compromise are needed to solve a problem. Stories can also model standing up for others safely, repairing hurt feelings after mistakes, and balancing honesty with kindness in sensitive conversations.
chunks.examples: A character apologises after interrupting a friend, works with the group to share limited resources, or chooses gentle honesty when giving feedback.

chunks.title: Play and Leisure Skills
chunks.content: Adventures should validate the importance of play, imagination, and creative leisure. Stories can encourage children to explore new hobbies, try cooperative games, and experiment with different roles in imaginative play. Leisure activities should be shown as places where children practice social skills, creativity, and problem-solving in low-stakes settings. Characters might balance active play with quiet time and learn to include others, respect boundaries, and handle disappointment in games fairly.
chunks.examples: Showing children inventing a game together, inviting a shy peer to join, or gracefully handling losing a turn-based contest.

chunks.title: Moral Foundations and Prosocial Behaviour
chunks.content: Stories should help children develop a simple but robust sense of right and wrong grounded in empathy, fairness, and responsibility. Plots should include clear but age-appropriate moral dilemmas that show how choices affect others, such as whether to tell the truth when it is uncomfortable, share limited resources, or stand up for someone being treated unfairly. Characters should model prosocial behaviour like helping, including, and protecting others, while also showing that mistakes can be repaired through apology, restitution, and changed behaviour.
chunks.examples: A child chooses to admit a mistake even though they fear getting in trouble, or shares a reward with a friend who helped them succeed.

chunks.title: Cognitive Growth, Metacognition, and Executive Functions
chunks.content: Stories should act as a playground for thinking skills. Challenges can require children to follow multi-step plans, hold information in mind, switch strategies when a plan fails, and inhibit impulsive reactions. Narratives can highlight characters who pause to think before acting, reflect on what worked or did not work, and adjust their approach next time. Puzzles and obstacles should be rooted in concrete logic suitable for ages 6–9 and can gently introduce metacognitive skills by having characters talk about how they are thinking or learning.
chunks.examples: A puzzle that requires remembering clues from earlier scenes, or a character who realises their first plan is not working and consciously tries a new strategy.

chunks.title: Sense of Humour and Growth Mindset
chunks.content: The template links a healthy sense of humour with a growth mindset. Stories should show humour as a way to play with ideas, rules, and expectations in a safe way, using jokes that are kind rather than cruel. At the same time, characters should encounter challenges that they cannot solve immediately and learn that effort, practice, and trying new strategies can lead to improvement. Failures and setbacks should be framed as normal parts of learning rather than as permanent verdicts on ability.
chunks.examples: A character makes a light-hearted joke about a mistake, then tries again and succeeds after practice, reinforcing both humour and perseverance.

chunks.title: Character Format and Roster Requirements
chunks.content: Every story must define a roster of 1 to 4 characters in YAML. Each character entry must include an id, a name, an image path, and optionally an audio path, along with a metadata block describing their role, archetype, species, age, personality traits, and short backstory. Characters should be designed to be distinct, appealing, and playable for children aged 6–9, with clear motivations and traits that can drive decisions in the story. The roster design must support fair distribution of meaningful decisions so that each child player can experience at least one important, scored moment in a session.
chunks.examples: A YAML character like Glimmerwing with unique traits and a role that clearly fits one of the Growth Echo archetypes.

chunks.title: Growth Echo Archetypes (Ages 6–9)
chunks.content: The character system uses five Growth Echo archetypes that represent different motivational patterns and growth challenges. Examples include: the_vanguard (Leadership and Order), who pushes the plot forward and wrestles with the line between justice and bossiness; the_architect (Security and Systems), who focuses on preparation and structure; the_pathfinder (Exploration and Curiosity); the_steward (Connection and Empathy); and the_innovator or inventor (Creativity and Ideas). Each archetype combines core capacities, such as leadership, fairness, planning, or communication, with a shadow side that defines its developmental edge. Characters should be assigned a primary archetype that guides how scenes present their strengths and growth opportunities.
chunks.examples: A captain-like character as a_vanguard who drives decisions but must learn flexibility, or a cautious planner as an_architect who must risk trying something new.

chunks.title: Smart Roster Model and Focus Choices
chunks.content: Each story is a thematic module for a specific age range and must be built around exactly three core compass axes chosen from a single Parent Group in the Master Badge Axis List. The Smart Roster model ensures that every character in the roster has exactly one Focus Choice dilemma associated with one of these axes. If there are four characters, there are four Focus Choices, distributed across the three axes so that different characters highlight different developmental themes. Focus Choices must be complex dilemmas between two positive values, such as Honesty versus Loyalty, rather than simple good-versus-bad questions. The private session setup links each child profile to a character, and the in-game experience must guarantee each player at least one scored interaction with their character, using clear prompts that call the child by name. Behind the scenes, the system records the outcomes of Focus Choices for later reflection and analysis.
chunks.examples: Four-character stories with four Focus Choices, each tied to one of three axes from a single Parent Group, ensuring every child is scored once per session.

chunks.title: Compass Axis and Scoring System
chunks.content: The compass axis system assigns numeric scores to choices that affect developmental axes. All such choices must use a compass_change event with a delta on a continuous scale from +1.0 (strongly positive) to -1.0 (strongly negative). Simple, low-stakes choices should use small deltas, while pivotal, emotionally complex moments may receive larger values. For complex dilemmas between two positive values, like Honesty versus Empathy, the player’s choice should produce a positive delta for the prioritised axis, and the alternative positive axis should not be penalised. Implementation requires attaching compass_change metadata to the relevant choice branches, naming the axis clearly and recording the delta so that cumulative scoring across scenes is possible.
chunks.examples: A dilemma where comforting a friend increases Empathy & Perspective-Taking, while telling a hard truth increases Honesty, with no negative score for the unchosen positive value.

chunks.title: Badge and Threshold System
chunks.content: The badge system tracks long-term individual progress across multiple games, separate from in-game group achievements. Only axes defined in the Master Badge Axis List are eligible for badge tracking. In any given story, a player can make progress only on the three axes featured in that module. Cross-game data collection aggregates compass_change scores for a single axis across different play sessions, and badges are awarded when a child’s cumulative score falls within defined threshold ranges for that axis. The three-tier badge structure allows for different levels of mastery or growth, reinforcing sustained patterns of prosocial choices rather than one-off actions.
chunks.examples: Awarding a first-tier badge after a child has consistently made honest choices across multiple stories that feature the Honesty & Integrity axis.

chunks.title: Compass Scoring Guide and developmental_link Key
chunks.content: The compass scoring guide defines how to assign delta values and document their developmental meaning. Every compass_change object must include a developmental_link key that cites the specific developmental concept from the alignment section (for example, a 2.1.x item). The developmental_link value is a string containing the reference number and name of the concept, making the pedagogical rationale explicit. Writers should analyse the choice, map it to the most relevant concept in the Master Badge Axis List, justify whether the impact is low, medium, or high, and then select a delta from the appropriate range. This creates a transparent workflow where narrative design, scoring, and developmental theory remain tightly connected.
chunks.examples: A compass_change for Empathy & Perspective-Taking that includes developmental_link: "2.1.2 Social Skills (Social Awareness & Relationship Skills)", with a delta calibrated to the impact of the choice.

chunks.title: Scene Types, Rolls, and Flow
chunks.content: The scene system distinguishes between narrative, choice, roll, and special scenes. All choice and roll scenes must include branches. Narrative Choices do not require dice and are used when players decide WHAT to do or say, such as where to go or whom to talk to. Action Choices are used when players decide HOW to attempt a meaningful challenge with uncertain outcome and must always lead directly to a Challenge Roll scene. Challenge Rolls determine success or failure of actions that carry consequences for the story and must follow a fixed structure: choice scene, then roll scene, then narrative outcome scenes for success and failure. Writers must ensure that each character has fair opportunities to shine across these structures. Flavor Rolls are low-stakes, clearly signposted random events that add humour and surprise without affecting main quest success; they must not introduce true failure or block story progression.
chunks.examples: Using an Action Choice to decide how to climb a cliff, followed by a d20 Challenge Roll and different success/failure outcomes; using a Flavor Roll to see what silly effect a magical gust of wind has.

chunks.title: Axis Scoring System for Cumulative Consequences
chunks.content: The axis scoring system for cumulative consequences provides an invisible assessment of a child’s behavioural pattern across a story. Writers choose one moral axis, such as Honesty & Integrity, to serve as a reputation score. Throughout the story, many small, low-impact compass_change events adjust this axis using modest positive or negative deltas, creating a cumulative total that reflects repeated choices rather than a single moment. At a key point, a special branch_on_axis_score scene checks the final score and routes the narrative toward a positive or challenging outcome. Immediately afterward, an Echo Reveal scene must explain why this outcome occurred, making the connection between prior behaviour and current consequences explicit and fair in the eyes of the player.
chunks.examples: A child who has repeatedly told the truth earns help from a guard because of their reputation, while a child who often lied faces a setback, with the Echo Reveal explaining the link to earlier choices.
